era_context: Late 15th century: Iberian Reconquista accelerating toward conclusion; Portuguese maritime exploration reaching southern Africa; Ottoman Empire consolidating Balkan and Mediterranean dominance; Ming Dynasty at height of centralized power; Aztec Empire under Ahuitzotl; African kingdoms engaged in expanding Atlantic trade; European Renaissance flourishing.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited European documentation; oral traditions underrepresented)

events.title: Bartolomeu Dias rounds Cape of Good Hope; Portuguese maritime breakthrough
events.region: Cape of Good Hope, Southern Africa
events.description: Portuguese navigator Bartolomeu Dias successfully rounds the Cape of Good Hope (Cabo da Boa Esperança), proving that a sea route to India is possible. This voyage represents a major breakthrough in Portuguese maritime exploration and opens the possibility of direct European access to Asian trade routes, bypassing Ottoman-controlled Mediterranean and Middle Eastern intermediaries. Dias's voyage is a critical step toward Vasco da Gama's later voyage to India (1498) and the establishment of European maritime dominance in the Indian Ocean. The achievement demonstrates Portuguese technological sophistication in navigation and ship design.

sources.name: Rui de Pina, Crónica de El-Rei D. João II (Portuguese royal chronicle, early 16th century)
Duarte Pacheco Pereira, Esmeraldo de Situ Orbis (Portuguese geographical account, early 16th century)
Diogo Gomes, De Prima Inventione Guinee (Portuguese account, 15th century)

events.certainty_note: Bartolomeu Dias's rounding of the Cape of Good Hope in 1488 is well-documented in Portuguese chronicles and later historiographic consensus. The date is firmly established.

events.title: Granada War continues; Christian forces advance toward final victory
events.description: The Granada War (1482–1492) continues with Christian forces under Ferdinand and Isabella maintaining military pressure on the Nasrid Emirate. By 1488, the Christian advance is accelerating, with key Nasrid strongholds falling to Christian forces. The Nasrid ruler Muhammad XII (Boabdil) is increasingly isolated and pressured to negotiate. This year represents a critical phase in the final Reconquista, with Christian victory appearing inevitable. The war's conclusion will mark the end of Islamic political power in Iberia.

sources.name: Andrés Bernáldez, Historia de los Reyes Católicos
Pulgar, Crónica de los Reyes Católicos

events.certainty_note: The Granada War's progression is well-documented in Spanish chronicles. The year 1488 is attested as a period of Christian military advance.

events.title: Ottoman-Venetian tensions escalate; naval conflicts in Eastern Mediterranean
events.description: Ottoman forces under Bayezid II continue to challenge Venetian naval and commercial dominance in the Eastern Mediterranean. By 1488, Ottoman military power is clearly superior to Venice's, and tensions are escalating. Ottoman corsairs and naval forces are increasingly active, threatening Venetian trade routes and colonial possessions. These tensions will culminate in the Fourth Venetian-Ottoman War (1499–1503), which will result in significant Venetian territorial losses and the shift of Mediterranean dominance to the Ottoman Empire.

sources.name: Ottoman court records and Turkish chronicles

events.certainty_note: Ottoman-Venetian tensions in this period are well-documented. Specific events in 1488 are less precisely dated.

events.title: Ming Dynasty maintains centralized control; Zheng He's treasure fleet operations continue
events.description: The Ming Dynasty under the Yongle Emperor's successors maintains centralized control and continues to support administrative and cultural institutions. By 1488, the Ming state represents one of the world's most sophisticated political systems. Zheng He's treasure fleet operations continue (though the major voyages ended around 1433, maintenance and preparation of the fleet continue). The Ming bureaucracy is highly developed, and cultural achievements in ceramics, painting, and literature are significant. This period represents the height of Ming stability and power.
key_figures: Yongle Emperor (Zhu Di) or his successors
events.certainty_note: Ming administrative and cultural achievements are well-documented in Ming records.

events.title: Ahuitzotl's reign continues; Aztec Empire at territorial zenith
events.description: Ahuitzotl (r. 1486–1502) continues his reign as the eighth Aztec emperor, maintaining the empire's territorial dominance and complex administrative systems. The Aztec state is characterized by sophisticated governance, extensive tribute networks, monumental architecture (including the expanded Templo Mayor), and a complex religious and social hierarchy. By 1488, the Aztec Empire is at its territorial maximum, with a population estimated at several million. The empire's power and stability are at their height.
events.certainty_note: Ahuitzotl's reign is well-attested in Aztec codices and Spanish colonial accounts. Specific events in 1488 are less precisely dated.

events.title: Italian Renaissance continues; major artistic achievements
events.description: The Italian Renaissance continues to flourish, with major artists and intellectuals producing significant works. Leonardo da Vinci, Michelangelo, and other Renaissance figures are at the height of their creative powers. Patronage from wealthy families and the Church supports extensive artistic and architectural projects. By 1488, the Renaissance represents a cultural transformation in Europe, with renewed interest in classical learning, humanism, and naturalistic representation.
events.certainty_note: Renaissance artistic achievements are well-documented in surviving artworks and manuscripts.

disconfirming_evidence: While Bartolomeu Dias's rounding of the Cape of Good Hope is a confirmed and significant achievement, its historical importance is partly retrospective. Dias's voyage did not immediately lead to European dominance in the Indian Ocean; that process took decades and involved complex interactions with established Asian maritime networks. The voyage is often presented as a 'breakthrough' that made European expansion inevitable, but this teleological framing obscures the contingency of historical development and the agency of Asian and African societies. Additionally, the characterization of Portuguese exploration as 'discovery' has been critiqued for erasing the knowledge and agency of African and Asian peoples.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1488 is rich for European, Ottoman, and Ming sources. African and Mesoamerican sources are mediated through later European compilation or oral tradition. The historical record privileges political, military, and cultural events. Gaps exist in documentation of economic, social, and environmental changes, and in non-elite perspectives. European-centric bias is evident in the emphasis on Portuguese exploration and the Reconquista. The significance of Dias's voyage is partly constructed by later historians emphasizing the prelude to European global dominance.

graph_edges.note: Dias's Cape rounding enables Vasco da Gama's voyage to India
Ottoman-Venetian tensions escalate into formal war
